# mRNA Manufacturing – The 5 process steps of mRNA production

![daniel-tischler](/uploads/media/blog_avatar/04/44-DATI.jpg?v=3-0 "daniel-tischler")

[Daniel Tischler](/author/daniel-tischler "Daniel Tischler")

July 5, 2023

Table of contents

mRNA manufacturing, or the production of messenger RNA (mRNA), has quickly become one of the most discussed topics in the biotechnology sphere. While the science of mRNA has been researched for quite some time, its application in drug manufacturing is a relatively recent development.

The novel use of [mRNA technology](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/nucleic-acids/mrna-technology-simply-explained) to instruct our body cells to perform specific tasks - such as creating proteins to combat diseases - has marked an exciting shift in the landscape of medicine. Particularly in the realms of [vaccine and therapeutic development](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/challenges-vaccine-development-manufacturing), the potential of mRNA technology has been vividly demonstrated, instigating a wave of promising research and applications.

This article unveils the intricate world of mRNA manufacturing. We examine its processes, progress, and future outlook, including the transformative role of single-use technology in facilitating scalability and enhancing efficiency.

## What is mRNA Manufacturing?

mRNA manufacturing involves the synthetic production of messenger RNA, a type of RNA that conveys genetic information from DNA to the machinery within cells responsible for protein synthesis. This process forms the basis of numerous biological functions, and harnessing it has opened up new avenues in biotechnology.

The ability to create mRNA molecules in a laboratory setting and use it to instruct host cells to produce specific proteins has the potential to revolutionize the way we treat diseases.

mRNA technology, the underpinning of mRNA manufacturing, employs this mechanism to fight diseases. By introducing laboratory-made nucleic acids into the body, scientists can essentially 'teach' cells how to fight off infections or diseases by targeted protein expression after cellular uptake. This has immense implications for the world of medicine and healthcare.

## The Impact of mRNA Vaccines

The impact of [mRNA vaccines](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/nucleic-acids/mrna-vaccines-safely-ship-store-them), particularly Covid-19 vaccines designed to combat the COVID-19 virus, has been monumental. mRNA vaccines like those developed by Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna have played a significant role in controlling the global pandemic.

Unlike traditional vaccine platforms, which introduce an inactivated or weakened virus into the body to stimulate an immune response, mRNA vaccines carry the genetic instructions that help human cells produce virus-like proteins. These proteins trigger the immune system, building a defensive response against the virus.

The rapid development and successful implementation of mRNA vaccines during the coronavirus pandemic showcased the potential of mRNA technology in tackling global health crises. It also highlighted the significance of maintaining robust mRNA production capabilities to respond swiftly to such outbreaks.

mRNA vaccines, through their quick production timelines and potency, have reshaped the landscape of vaccine development and deployment globally.

By successfully controlling the pandemic in many parts of the world, mRNA vaccines have demonstrated the power of biotechnology and mRNA manufacturing, ushering in a new era of disease control and prevention.

![The Impact of mRNA Vaccines against COVID-19](/uploads/media/default/03/1583-mrna-vaccines-covid-19.jpg?v=1-0 "The Impact of mRNA Vaccines against COVID-19")

## Considerations in mRNA Manufacturing

Navigating the pathway of mRNA manufacturing involves a number of considerations beyond the primary biological procedures. These revolve around product quality, regulatory compliance, and adaptability to therapeutic needs.

### Key Factors in Manufacturing Therapeutics

The manufacturing of mRNA-based therapeutics needs to be flexible and adaptable to the needs of different therapies. This includes considerations such as

### Regulatory and Quality Considerations

Biologics and [vaccine manufacturing](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/vaccine-manufacturing), including mRNA, requires strict adherence to regulatory guidelines and [Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP)](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/manufacturing-processes/biopharma/cgmp-everything-need-know). This ensures that the final product, be it an mRNA-based vaccine or therapeutic, is of high quality and safe for use. Regulatory considerations extend across all stages of mRNA production, including [plasmid DNA manufacturing](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/nucleic-acids/plasmid-manufacturing-process-simply-explained), mRNA synthesis, and purification. Maintaining rigorous quality control at every step is critical to achieving a product that meets all required specifications.

## 5 mRNA Production Steps

The simplicity, scalability, and speed of mRNA production make it a promising platform for drug development. This is particularly true for the production of vaccines, where speed is often of the essence. Unlike traditional vaccine development methods, which may take years, mRNA vaccines can be developed in a matter of months.

### Step 1: mRNA Template Design

The first step of the manufacturing process of mRNA therapeutics starts with the design of a plasmid DNA (pDNA) template, which consists of an RNA polymerase promoter and a specific mRNA sequence. After a plasmid DNA template has been established, it is amplified by using expression systems like e.Coli bacteria, for example. For enhanced integration, the plasmid DNA template is linearized.

To trigger the desired immune response and lead the cells to produce antibodies against a disease, this step has to be carried out with absolute accuracy. To ensure the stability of the sequence encoding the desired antigen, gene expression and translational qualities, it is important to protect it from any impurities.

### Step 2: In vitro Transcription (IVT)

Following the design and preparation of the DNA template, the linear plasmid DNA has to be transcribed into mRNA. This is usually done by using in vitro transcription (IVT). In contrast to in vivo transcription, the mRNA is produced without using raw materials from animals. The synthesis of RNA is achieved through an enzymatic reaction of RNA polymerase and nucleotides.

After transcription, the mRNA has to be capped, to protect it from nuclease degradation.1 This takes place in the nucleus, where the mRNA capping enzyme equips the mRNA with a group of molecules that help keep the mRNA stable and enhance its translational and exporting qualities. There is also the option of co-transcription capping, which is less cost intensive than enzymatic capping, but also shows less effectivity in comparison.

These steps in mRNA modification are essential not only to enhance stability and translatability, but also to reduce immunogenicity.[1](#citation1 "mRNA capping: biological functions and applications")

### Step 3: mRNA purification

As the DNA template is no longer needed after transcription, it has to be removed from the mRNA product. To isolate the mRNA, biotechnology has developed a number of strategies. mRNA can be purified through different chromatography steps and filtration techniques that use magnetic beads or affinity purification. For large scale purification chromatographic strategies are coupled with tangential flow filtration (TFF).2

The purification process is not only necessary to separate mRNA from the plasmid DNA template, but to further remove any impurities that could hinder the mRNA production process.[2](#citation2 "mRNA vaccines manufacturing: Challenges and bottlenecks")

### Step 4: mRNA Characterization

There are certain risk factors involved in the production of mRNA which call for efficient analytical tools to ensure that the mRNA product is not altered and safe for patients to use. During process development, the mRNA can degrade due to changes in temperature, exposure to light or ribonucleases. To minimize the threat of product alterations and impurities, different techniques for mRNA characterization have been developed to assess its quality. Among these methods are mass-spectrometry, RNase mapping and RNA-sequence mapping.3

With the emergence of mRNA in vaccine production during the recent coronavirus pandemic, mRNA technology has experienced a massive increase in interest. The development of two Covid-19-vaccines by Moderna and Biontech/Pfizer, shone the spotlight on mRNA-based vaccines. There are currently a number of mRNA vaccines for the treatment of infectious diseases in clinical trials or awaiting their FDA approval. Other components like viral vectors or lipid nanoparticles (LNPs)  that act as mRNA delivery systems have to be considered during mRNA vaccine development as well, as they have a strong influence on biological efficacy.[3](#citation3 "Characterization and Sequence Mapping of Large RNA and mRNA Therapeutics Using Mass Spectrometry")[4](#citation4 "The Storage and In-Use Stability of mRNA Vaccines and Therapeutics: Not A Cold Case")

### Step 5: Formulation and Stability Optimization

mRNA therapeutic and vaccine candidates have to face one of mRNA’s biggest challenges, which is its low stability. In order to prevent mRNA degradation, mRNA products require frozen storage, but also protective measures during mRNA manufacturing.

Many mRNA therapeutics and vaccines use LNPs and other lipids or carbohydrates which help to deliver the drug safely to the cells of the immune system. Further, LNPs can help to stabilize the mRNA formulation. After formulation, the drug product is sterilized, aseptically filled and packaged for further storage or transport.[5](#citation5 "The Storage and In-Use Stability of mRNA Vaccines and Therapeutics: Not A Cold Case")

![mrna-manufacturing-process](/uploads/media/default/04/1614-mrna-manufacturing-process.png?v=1-0 "mrna production steps")

## mRNA Manufacturing Strategies

When it comes to mRNA manufacturing, various strategies have been developed and refined. They have to be adapted to the specific use of the subsequent mRNA product, such as vaccines or gene therapies, and leverage the unique capabilities of mRNA manufacturing. Nevertheless, common steps in the manufacturing of mRNA products include formulation, the creation of the drug product, and clinical trials.

### mRNA for Vaccine Production

The production of mRNA vaccines involves designing lipid nanoparticles that effectively deliver the mRNA to cells. It is crucial that the encapsulated mRNA generates the right protein, eliciting the production of antibodies to fight the pathogen.

mRNA vaccines against SARS-CoV-2, developed by companies like Pfizer and Moderna, offer examples of this strategy. These vaccines introduced the genetic code of the virus' spike protein to the body, stimulating an immune response. This approach of vaccine technology has proven successful, with the vaccines demonstrating high efficacy in clinical trials.

### mRNA for Gene Therapy and Immune Response

mRNA's role isn't limited to vaccine production. It's also a promising tool in gene therapy, offering a potentially safer modality to DNA-based methods. By delivering genetic information directly to the body's cells, mRNA can stimulate the production of therapeutic proteins, potentially treating a range of genetic diseases.

mRNA also plays a pivotal role in evoking immune responses, thereby contributing to the development of therapies for infectious diseases. In this context, mRNA acts as a platform technology, where the same manufacturing process can be used to produce therapies for different diseases.

## Challenges and Bottlenecks in mRNA Drug Manufacturing

Despite the manifold possibilities offered by mRNA, manufacturing poses several challenges to stakeholders. Scaling up mRNA drug manufacturing to meet global demand presents one of them, along with process optimization and quality control issues.

### Scaling-Up mRNA Manufacturing

Increasing production volumes while ensuring product quality is a complex task. Challenges encompass securing consistent supplies of raw materials, maintaining the quality of [drug substance](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/vaccines/safe-handling-bulk-drug-substances), and refining various steps in the process for efficiency and cost-effectiveness. The ability to adapt rapidly to changing demands and emerging disease threats adds an additional layer of complexity, requiring high process flexibility.

### Quality Control and Optimization

Despite the urgency of scale-up, strict quality control measures must not be compromised. From controlling the absence of contaminants to ensuring the final product meets all specifications, the quality control process needs to be rigorous and precise from small scale to large scale. Accurate fluid management is essential for ensuring quality and effectiveness of the final mRNA product.2

Aseptic filling, a crucial step in the production chain, can pose significant contamination risks. Further, maintaining a consistent cold chain, particularly for mRNA vaccines that require [ultra-low storage temperatures](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/manufacturing-processes/freeze-thaw/ultra-cold-freezing-storing-considerations-solutions), is another significant challenge. This necessitates the deployment of innovative technologies capable of ensuring temperature stability throughout the product's lifecycle.

These challenges, while substantial, also provide opportunities for innovation and advancement in mRNA drug manufacturing. This is exactly where Single Use Support comes in with its innovative [single-use systems](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/manufacturing-processes/single-use-technology/single-use-technology-implementation-biopharma).[6](#citation6 "mRNA vaccines manufacturing: Challenges and bottlenecks")

## Single Use Support's mRNA Manufacturing Solutions

Stepping up to the challenges related to mRNA manufacturing, Single Use Support offers automated closed systems based on [single-use technology designed for mRNA manufacturing](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/nucleic-acids/single-use-technology-mrna-manufacturing). These solutions address critical aspects of the process, including filling & aliquoting and freeze & thaw processes.

Single Use Support's RoSS.FILL platform allows for fully automated and aseptic [filling & aliquoting of mRNA](https://www.susupport.com/knowledge/biopharmaceutical-products/drug-delivery/filling-filtering-dispensing-mrna), adhering to stringent cGMP regulations. Its modularity allows for unlimited scalability and process versatility. Additionally, their advanced freeze & thaw platforms ensure that mRNA or Lipid Nanoparticles (LNPs) are handled in a controlled manner, mitigating risks associated with temperature fluctuations.

![RoSS.FILL_Sealer-Stepper Valve Rack_2](/uploads/media/default/03/723-RoSS.FILL%20CGT_Product%20photo%20%2822%20von%2026%29.jpg?v=3-0 "RoSS.FILL_Sealer-Stepper Valve Rack_2")

Their solutions extend to the cold chain storage and shipping of mRNA substances too. Frozen single-use bags are housed in a protective single-use shell and can be securely stored in a cold storage freezer. For shipping, RoSS.SHIP offers stable temperature control for at least six days, ensuring that the integrity and cold chain of the mRNA substances is maintained.

Single Use Support's end-to-end solutions pave the way for a streamlined and robust mRNA manufacturing process, contributing significantly to the field's rapid advancement.

App Note: The Chilled Future of RNA Therapeutics Filling & Freezing Applications

## Outlook for mRNA drug manufacturing

The achievements in mRNA therapeutics have solidified mRNA's position as a revolutionary force in the pharmaceutical industry. Looking forward, mRNA drug manufacturing is poised to catalyze advancements in diverse therapeutic areas, beyond infectious diseases.

As techniques continue to improve and our understanding of the underlying biology deepens, we can anticipate the development of mRNA-based therapies for genetic disorders, cancers, autoimmune diseases, and more.

In addition, continual innovations in single-use technologies and automated systems will further streamline mRNA manufacturing processes, improving scalability, efficiency, and quality control.

It is essential to remember that success in mRNA drug manufacturing will require overcoming the complex challenges and bottlenecks we've explored in this article. The role of pioneering process solution providers, such as Single Use Support, will only grow in importance as we step into this exciting future.

![MilliporeSigma](/static/logos/purple/millipore_sigma.svg)

# mRNA Synthesis for the Development of Vaccines and Therapeutics

**Section Overview**

The potential for synthetic mRNA to recapitulate the function of mature mRNA has elevated its potential for a spectrum of biopharmaceutical applications. These therapeutics include *in vivo* delivery of mRNA for protein replacement, stem cell induction, or cancer immunotherapies. Recent significant pharmaceutical uses include novel approaches for vaccines against infectious disease, most notably the mRNA-based vaccines that protect against SARS-CoV-2.

This technical article focuses on pDNA and mRNA preparation for lab-scale applications. For a complete overview of our products and solutions suitable for GMP manufacturing, visit our [mRNA Manufacturing and Formulation page](/US/en/integrated-offerings/mrna-development-manufacturing/mrna-manufacturing-formulation).

## How mRNA Vaccines Induce Immunity

Messenger RNA (mRNA)-based vaccines are derived from the central dogma that mRNA encodes for proteins. This vaccine modality is straightforward, inducing an immunological response by delivering a genetic element that codes for all (or part) of an antigenic protein in a “translation-ready” molecule. This process is facilitated when the mRNA encoding the target antigen is taken up by antigen-presenting cells and translated into the target protein. This in turn induces an immune response, closely mimicking the natural process by which viruses infect cells.

## Advantages of mRNA as a Vaccine Modality

Generally, mRNA vaccines are produced by *in vitro* synthesis through an enzymatic process. The development and manufacture of mRNA vaccines are relatively simple compared to more traditional modalities and can be accomplished in a shorter time period. These attributes make them suitable for accelerated development and scaleup, and are critical for emergent public health scenarios. Future innovations in mRNA vaccine design, formulation, and enhanced thermostability (beyond cold-chain distribution) would further reduce production costs and enhance global accessibility of mRNA as a modality.

## Plasmid DNA (pDNA) Preparation for mRNA Synthesis

For mRNA-based vaccine design, *in vitro* transcription of a plasmid DNA (pDNA) template is typically used to produce functional synthetic mRNA. The plasmid vector may contain the following elements: an upstream promoter exclusively recognized by [T7](/US/en/product/sigma/rt7pol), [SP6](/US/en/product/roche/rpolsp6ro), or [T3](/US/en/product/roche/rpolt3ro) RNA polymerase, all of which are derived from bacteriophages; 5' UTR; cDNA; 3' UTR; a downstream poly A-tail; and a unique cleavage site downstream of the poly A-tail. Preparation of the pDNA template often involves [PCR amplification](/US/en/product/sigma/kmm101nv) and gel purification of your target DNA, followed by restriction digestion (of both insert and plasmid backbone) and ligation using enzymes such as [T4 DNA Ligase](/US/en/product/mm/69839m). Selection of proper transformation reagents ([BL21 competent cells](/US/en/product/mm/69449), [SOC broth](/US/en/product/sigma/s1797), and [LB-agar plates](/US/en/product/sigma/l5542) with selection antibiotics) and purification techniques ([GenElute™ Plasmid Miniprep Kit](/US/en/product/sigma/pln70)) are all considerations as researchers optimize conditions for template generation.

![Steps in plasmid generation for in vitro transcription (IVT).](/deepweb/assets/sigmaaldrich/marketing/global/images/technical-documents/articles/pharma-and-biopharma-manufacturing/vaccine-manufacturing/plasmid-workflow/plasmid-workflow.jpg "Steps in plasmid generation for in vitro transcription (IVT).")

**Figure 1.**Plasmid generation for *in vitro* transcription (IVT). Following gene synthesis, cloning of the target DNA uses a plasmid vector (pDNA). The pDNA is expanded in bacterial culture, then purified using nucleic acid purification methods, such as silica-based membranes in spin columns.

## mRNA *IN VITRO* TRANSCRIPTION AND CAPPING

Bacteriophage RNA polymerase is normally used to transcribe linearized plasmid DNA. The pDNA is first linearized with the selected unique restriction site enzyme. After digestion, the linearized pDNA may be purified using methods such as the phenol-chloroform protocol or the [GenElute™ PCR Clean-Up Kit](/US/en/product/sigma/na1020). For large scale purification, tangential flow filtration (TFF) is often advisable as it can be easily scaled up. Following linearization, *in vitro* transcription and capping is performed in a mixed solution of recombinant RNA polymerase ([T7](/US/en/product/sigma/rt7pol), [SP6](/US/en/product/roche/rpolsp6ro) or [T3](/US/en/product/roche/rpolt3ro)) and nucleoside triphosphates ([ATP](/US/en/product/sigma/a6559), [CTP](/US/en/product/sigma/c8552), [GTP](/US/en/product/sigma/g3776), [UTP](/US/en/product/sigma/u1006)), plus a cap analog such as CleanCap® Reagent or ARCA (Anti-Reverse Cap Analog). A modified nucleoside such as N1-Methylpseudouridine-5'-Triphosphate (N1-Methylpseudo-UTP, 1-Methylpseudo-UTP) can be used instead of GTP to suppress the innate immune system and is used in the current mRNA vaccines. The efficiency of capping mRNA with ARCA is ~70-80% on average because it competes with GTP, while the yield of mRNA is reduced by about one-fourth compared to standard mRNA synthesis. In comparison, the CleanCap® reagent has been shown to work at 94% capping efficiency without affecting the yield of mRNA production. Capping may also be achieved post-transcriptionally, without a cap analog, by employing the vaccinia virus-encoded capping complex (Vaccinia capping enzyme, 2'-O-Methyltransferase, GTP, and S-adenosyl methionine (SAM)). Capping efficiency will depend on the secondary structure of the mRNA of interest. Finally, the length of the poly A tail of the template pDNA (up to 150 bases) can be extended by use of poly A enzyme when necessary.

### Plasmid Preparation Reagents

![Steps in the synthesis of mRNA](/deepweb/assets/sigmaaldrich/marketing/global/images/technical-documents/articles/pharma-and-biopharma-manufacturing/vaccine-manufacturing/steps-synthesis/steps-synthesis.jpg "Steps in the synthesis of mRNA")

**Figure 2.**mRNA synthesis is completed by linearization of pDNA, *in vitro* transcription (IVT) of mRNA using cell-free methods, and capping of the mRNA using cap analog or virus-encoded capping complex.

## mRNA Synthesis Reagents for *In Vitro* Transcription (IVT)

### Transcription Buffers

## PURIFICATION AND ANALYSIS OF mRNA

The first step in mRNA purification is to remove the linearized pDNA template using deoxyribonuclease, or DNase. The mRNA is then precipitated with lithium chloride ([LiCl](/US/en/product/sigma/l7026)), and washed with 75% ethanol. The resulting mRNA pellet can be resuspended with water or resuspension buffer. However, this precipitation method should be avoided during scale-up given the overall difficulty and use of hazardous solvents, which should be avoided under GMP production. As a standardized solution, the [GenElute™ mRNA Miniprep Kit](/US/en/product/sigma/mrn70) is recommended for smaller scale purification.

## REMOVAL OF dsRNA DURING mRNA SYNTHESIS

Double-stranded RNA (or dsRNA) is a transcriptional by-product and a major concomitant of mRNA synthesis. The dsRNA can stimulate innate immune responses that counteract and reduce the translation efficiency of delivered mRNA. When mRNA is synthesized for vaccine development, dsRNA should therefore be removed. Cellulose-based purification using [cellulose fibers](/US/en/product/sigma/c6288) can be applied for the removal of dsRNA at various scales via chromatography.

# Innovative mRNA Production Techniques for Efficient RNA Drug Development

* [modalities](https://www.aldevron.com/modalities)
* RNA Design & Development

## Enhancing mRNA production begins with stability at the design stage

Imagine having access to a comprehensive range of specialized products and services for mRNA production that can significantly reduce supply-chain risks, shorten lead times, and enhance quality. Whether you need simple starting material such as cloning backbones, enzymes for mRNA synthesis in various configurations from research grade to cGMP drug substance and product, or specific options for capping, tailing, and chemical modification, you can find everything you need to support your program development. mRNA production can begin with an electronic DNA template sequence or supercoiled plasmid DNA provided by the client, supported by process development, scale-up, and analytics required to move your project into clinical trials and beyond.

## MRNA construct design and optimization

Aldevron's state-of-the-art quality systems ensure your material meets your specifications. From cloning, verification, restriction digestion, strain selection, cell banking, and plasmid preparation, we meticulously manage every step to guarantee the integrity and efficacy of the mRNA sequence. Whether for research or clinical applications, our commitment to quality ensures the mRNA you receive is of the utmost reliability.

**RNA Backbones for Vaccine and Therapeutic Development**

* Optimized for streamlining the development of vaccines and therapeutics
* Constructs feature a T7 promoter for RNA production
* Proprietary 5’ UTR sequence to help determine which configuration works best
* Options include encoded poly(A) region

**Strain Selection and Optimization**

* Test 2-3 *E.coli* strains
* Generate a design space for process conditions, including multiple temp and media components
* Develop a small-scale model for full-scale production

**cGMP Master Cell Bank Generation**

* Generate cell bank for use in research and cGMP projects
* Enable faster project execution during late-stage clinical phases

## LNP formulation

LNP formulation is important for long-term success of any RNA drug product. We partner with Cytiva and Acuitas to work with you on LNP formulation. These partnerships and capabilities assure that you have access to all the essentials for mRNA vaccine and therapeutic development, including:

* Encapsulation technology and expertise
* A lipid library as an initial step for screening
* Multiple platforms to optimize consistency and encapsulation efficiency
* Scale based to support various stages of development The Aldevron Advantage

Providing our clients with options, from the simplest starting material to the most complex manufacturing operation, is part of our culture. Our aim is to bring innovation to each step of the journey.

### Cloning backbones

Clone your gene of interest into one of several [Aldevron backbones](/catalog-products/cloning-backbones/pald-utr-rna), some with optimized UTRs, for efficient mRNA production.

### Alchemy™ Cell-free DNA

Reduce linear DNA manufacturing times by over 50% and reduce host cell contamination with the [Alchemy](/custom-manufacturing/alchemy-cell-free-dna-technology) Cell-free DNA enzymatic manufacturing process

### Plasmid DNA and linearization

Produce phase-appropriate plasmid DNA for research through clinical applications at a variety of scales. Recover 90-95% linear DNA with consistently high poly(A) tails through optimized linearization and purification protocols.

### Process development and scale up

Utilize experience gained from over 130 cGMP RNA lots produced for clinical trials and commercial applications to optimize process and scale for each unique molecule.

### RNA drug substance manufacturing

Flexible, scalable process [produces high-quality RNA](/modalities/rna/rna-drug-substance) tailored to your specifications, delivering quantities from milligrams to multi-grams.

### RNA LNP drug product

Seamless continuation of the [RNA drug product manufacturing](/modalities/rna/rna-drug-product) process, incorporating LNP encapsulation, analytical testing and sterile fill/finish under a single roof and quality system.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The initial step involves designing a plasmid DNA (pDNA) template, essential for synthesizing mRNA, ensuring accurate representation of the desired antigen sequence for effective therapeutic outcomes.
Integrating a specific RNA polymerase promoter into the pDNA template is crucial for initiating mRNA transcription, facilitating the expression of targeted proteins in host cells.
Post-design, the pDNA is amplified through bacterial culture and purified, removing impurities to ensure high-quality, functional mRNA production through subsequent in vitro transcription.
